mind me sharing the story, but Kirk and I had a phone call like six months ago, Kirk. Yeah? Yeah, six months ago, exactly. And Kirk was basically at between a rock and a hard place. And it was like, my model is just, it's not profitable. It's not making money. I'm going to lose like $5,000 this month. How can I handle this? And the process that I took him through was me just kind of free-balling, what am I about to say? So this is a much cleaner, more methodical, sequential way of taking you guys through the process that I took. Kirk through, but what did we cut your overhead from, too? Yeah, we cut payroll by 65%. And then we went from, I think you were like negative, like $2,000 or $3,000 a month to like positive, what? OK, I can't hear him. To positive $5,000. That's cool, right? Just by moving math, right? I think that's interesting. Cool, so we'll talk about it. OK, so during the last presentation, the entire model should have felt very simplified. Did it feel simplified with the number? You know what your critical number is. That's what you're going to do all day, every day, until you get bored. And then you keep doing it and get more bored. Thank you. So I had some announcements I was supposed to make before this. Well, something very cool. Did you guys know there's seven countries represented here? Isn't that cool? OK, the second thing was that does everyone have the ribbons? And everyone understand the ribbons this time? I went from like 20 to like 3 and tried to make it real simple for everybody. I wanted you guys to have those and mention it before I forgot. That's why I have this. Because when we do dinner tonight, I'd recommend you pair up with people who don't have the same ribbons as you. So if there's a whole bunch of you guys who are really good at retention, you all don't need to talk. You know what I mean? Talk to people who are good at sales if you're good at retention. Or if you're good at high ticket stuff and you're not good at other stuff, then talk to those people. But this way it should give you a really good idea of what people's one thing that they're good at. And then you can ask them about those things. Cool, right? I thought it would facilitate conversation. And if you see any of the black ones that say seven figure gym, it's because they have a seven figure gym. So if you look at those, then definitely listen to what those people are saying. Cool? Oh, shoot. And the last one. So if everyone, so everyone enjoyed Lyndon's presentation? And I tried to balance the presentations, like I said earlier, with a little bit less intense stuff so that mine could be broken up. And so this one's going to be one of the more intense ones. So just buckle up, enjoy. So people are always really amazed at how simple our model is. So when I go to different events, people hear the revenue levels that we're hitting and they're like, holy crap, what are you guys doing? Let me see your funnel. And I'm like, we got two pages? No, we have three pages. We have a schedule page too. And they're like, what do you mean? They're like, no, that's really simple. We just sell the first thing and then we sell another thing after that. And then we just try and keep them. That's it. It's because simple works. Simple works, that's kind of what it is. Netflix has one membership and they just try and put so much stuff in there that no one ever leaves. So anyways, clarity is the precursor to focus. And so we kind of cover the clarity in terms of what your goals are. So everyone right now should have an idea of what their EFT goal should be in order to make the money that they want to make right now. Cool? Great. And you should have it broken down to how many leads per day you need to be generating and what you need to do in order to achieve that. Cool. And everyone knows that they will focus on converting leads into sales and EFTs and retaining customers up until this point. I feel like, what is that movie? Mean Girls? When he's like, all right, everybody take it over. No? OK, just me. I always think that's funny. OK. The reason I'm so enthralled by this simple model is that it simplifies the acquisition process and allows everyone to focus on doing one thing. So the reason I'm so in favor of the green play, one, obviously there's lifetime value, which is just better and more and you make more money and then you can sleep at night because you're recurring revenue makes you all of your money plus all your bills, which is kind of the goal. But from a managing your team standpoint, you only have two types of people, prospects and customers, rather than prospects, almost customers and customers. And what ends up happening most of the time invariably is that we have prospects close to customers where we give all of our attention to because they're challengers and then customers who are the real ones who decided to say yes, who then we inevitably neglect. I'm not saying everybody does, but a lot of people can fall into that trap. And so the reason that I'm so in favor and so bullish on the green play is that if you just have leads walking the door, which hopefully should be automated, hey, if you haven't signed up for ALN, alexislink.com, I think it's funny. Then once they turn into customers and you have the green play, it's like, that's it. And now it's just fulfillment. And then that's all you have to think about. And so I just think my prediction is that within the next nine months, I think at least half of this room will be doing over $50,000 a month in recurring, not just in total sales. Because if you just look at the math when I was saying earlier how we stacked our revenue and it just took time for it to get there, that's all we're doing is we're just putting money into the future bank. And once we, if everyone here commits to doing that and commits to signing up 30, 40 EFTs every single month, as long as you, like, as long as fulfillment is not absolute shit, you'll have a pretty, really like a very profitable gym by the end. And so what I'm going to break down is if you do get to that full capacity like Justin, how we can make it even more profitable without getting, like, to get your, I'm trying not to use inappropriate term, like get your jollies off on like this kind of stuff rather than like another gym or like a meal prep service. OK. So here's the fundamental mindset shift that you're going to need to have in order to like really make more money. And this was something that was a singe moment for me. And it was focusing, like, a lot of people, especially in the beginning when they started in gym lunch, they focus on making a customer to get a sale rather than making a sale to get a customer. And so like the whole point of like that first transaction is just to get them in the door so that you can begin the amazement process, not to get their money so that you now have the sale. I know it sounds fundamentally like a small nuance, but hopefully you can hear the difference. OK, hopefully you can. And so the next shift that I want you to make is about your current model and how profitable it really is. Because if we can improve this, then we can increase the speed and the ease that you hit that profit number that we're talking about earlier. So I told you to make that number based on where you're at now, which is the first presentation. This will help take where it now is closer. OK. So everybody likes things fast and easy? Cool. OK, so take out your homework numbers again. And these are the numbers that we need this time. So I don't need your bank account numbers and the expense numbers and any of that stuff. That should already have been covered so we can set our goals. Numbers that I need now are your KPIs. So the key performance indicators, how many EFTs you have, your average price of your EFT, which, for example, if you're like, I have nine memberships and they're all the same, but they're all $20 more than one another. Some of you are like this. We have our plus membership, which is $129, and our plus plus, which is $149 a month. And then this other thing that we measure once a week, you're like, ugh. Anyways, so just take all of them, divide it by how many people you have and that's your average EFT. Cap of each session, space-wise, that's a facility question. Cost of your admin, cost of your training, like the actual fulfillment training of your sessions. The number of sessions you have a month or a week, and then what your membership packages are. All right, those are the only variables that we're going to use, and those are the only variables that I used with Kirk, and that's how we took him from losing $3,000 a month to making $5,000 a month just by moving numbers. Cool? OK. Why? Because there are five levers to profit. So what this is, I have an infographic book coming out soon. Is that cool? Thank you. I worked really long on it. Really long on it. I did. And so that's one of them, and there's 18 of them. And they all have a video explanation and transcribed. It's very nifty. I'm going to be giving away for free. And you guys can have the first copies. But this is like the five levers to profit in the gym, and we'll explore each one of them in more depth. But we're going to be going through what type of membership you're offering from a fulfillment standpoint, the price points for that fulfillment that are optimal in my opinion, the billing cycles that are ideal for maximizing profit and for ease of running your gym, the actual fulfillment in terms of how many sessions per day, how many people per session, and how to structure those sessions, and then how many sessions you're doing over a week. OK. And before I tell you this story, so this is like before I get the nitty-gritty, I'm trying to put stories in it. And I'm like, fluff it up a little bit. Is that before I tell you this story, it's kind of a sound issue, how many people will only adopt this type of thinking when their backs are against the wall? And so I would implore you to not wait until you're about to be broke to then decide to start making money. But just maybe be making some money now and make way more. But it's up to you. So don't be like them and adopt it when you're ahead and make more. So here's the first story just to illustrate my point. And this is kind of like me kind of a little bit pontificating on CrossFit in general. Actually, I'm not beating you guys up. I'm just saying the model's a little bit broken. And so this is Josh Price, which is so appropriate, because he's not here. Can someone take a picture of this real quick? For all the people. He's like, he was the second person to ever buy Jim Launch, and he's been an OG since the beginning. But Josh Price, for those of you who don't know, when my mom got really sick, when I had my gyms, I had to go back east coast, which is where she was. My gyms were on the west coast. And I didn't sit for my gym for like, I think, six or eight months. It was kind of a blur. But I didn't talk to anyone. Like, I literally just left. I just walked out on my gyms. And when I was there, a friend of mine was like, hey, do you think you could help Josh out? He's a friend of mine from back home. Josh's not a friend of mine. Brian's a friend of mine. Brian told me about Josh. And I was like, yeah, sure, why not? And so I pull up to Josh's. Actually, I call him a bunch of times. And he doesn't respond, of course. And then finally, he's like, yeah, sure, I'd love help. And I was like, cool, what's your ad budget? And he's like, I don't have one. He's like, I'm actually broke. And I was like, oh, OK, cool. I was like, OK, well, I'll spend my own money. And I'll just help you out, because you're a friend of Brian's. And he's like, OK, I can sell between 12 and 2. But I'll have my two-year-old, my two-month-old with me. Hope that's cool. And I was like, that's not going to work. So if you know Josh, you know that's exactly how he would have said it. He would have been like, that's cool, right? I was like, no, that's not cool. No one can meet with you on Tuesdays and Thursdays between 12 and 2. So anyways, I ended up asking him, do you have CrossFit, CrossFit WSA T-shirt and hat? And he was like, yeah, I got that. I was like, well, I'm going to work your front desk. So I ended up flying out and working Josh's front desk while my mom was six. So I could take my mind off stuff. So anyways, when I got there, so I'm in Baltimore, ready to launch Josh's gym. And this was the first ever launch. I get there. And John, who is his partner at the time, they're no longer partners, was like, yeah, we're not going to do it. I was like, you're broke. Like, what do you have to lose? And it took me four hours to get them to agree to let me to spend my money and my time to sell them people into their gym. Yeah, it was a hardcore close. I'm like, some people just don't want to make money. So anyways, so I'm there with Josh. And during this four-hour time span, the thing that nailed them in or nailed my point down, let me see if I got this, OK, was that they were at full capacity. So they did a 3,000 square facility in Baltimore. Probably 2,500 minimum of that was probably more than that. Yeah, about 2,500 was usable space. And they had a max per session count of 13 people. And I was like, what do you mean? They're like, we can't even handle the thing that you're going to be sending us. So I was like, hold on, hold on. I was like, so you're broke. And I was like, and you have a model that's at full capacity and you don't make profit. I was like, you see any problem here? You're full, and you're not making money. I was like, you picking up on a buying? I was like, you picking up on a putting it down here? And I had to say it like four different ways until like, so you can't grow anymore, but you're not making money. I was like, you've got a two-year-old, and you can't grow your business. And then eventually they're like, I think he's on to something. It's exactly how Josh would have said it. Anyways, what I ended up having to do was I grabbed the jump rope. And it was like this very dramatic scene. You know, just imagine this dude who flies in all whatever with a mustache. And so I go in front of him and John, and I was like, this jump rope, I was like, is strangling your business. I was like, this jump rope is $10. And I was like, and it's going to put you out of business if you keep using it. And they were like, OK. And I was like, OK. So your double-unders are cutting the usage space of your capacity in half, which means you can't have more members at your gym, which means you can't make no money. And I was like, for a $10 jump rope. I was like, you following me, right? And they're like, OK, I think he's saying that we should not do jump ropes. I was like, sort of. Yes, we should think about how we're using our space. And it's one of the issues that with CrossFit, and I'm not picking on CrossFit specific, that's their example. Is that there's a lot of movements that they do. That A, for general population, aren't necessary. And B, just from an economy of space standpoint, just don't make sense. It uses way too much space. And if there's other ways of accomplishing the same objective of general conditioning, if you have like, well, I've got a competitor. It's like, cool, tell him to do double-unders on his own time. You know what I mean? Like, if he's really trying to practice the skill of double-unders and he's a competitor, then he doesn't need to do them during class anyways. They're going to be doing like, so don't disrupt the class. Make it appropriate for everyone. And if the goal is conditioning, there's a million ways we can do it without eating up 100 square feet for one person. So that's sort of the moral of the story, just in terms of how to think about your profitability. And they were obviously selling unlimited memberships for $99 a month. And everyone was bitching about how expensive it was. And then you know what to do. So that's the classic story. I think they had 100 members at, actually, I think it was set. They were making 7,000 a month off 100 members, is what it worked out to. And he had to basically just do one-on-one training in order to feed himself. And that just paid rent. So that's them now. And hopefully, the story kind of illustrates the point of why that model is broken and how yours might be in the same. Because some of you guys are newer to this. Some of you got the people who are new here and who raised their hand yesterday. Yesterday. Woo. This morning. That this was your first time at one of these events. If right now you are in that situation, you're like, I'm kind of close to capacity. You might not necessarily need to be losing money. But if you're only making $3,000 a month, then you're at cap, then something's got to change. You don't have to change the model. And that also means you're going to have to have a couple of difficult conversations. That's all it really comes down to. And then you'll be able to make more money. So here's some of the fundamental tweaks that we can make to the model to make it significantly more profitable. And the good news is that the happy ending for Josh is that he ended up buying John out. And they went from 7,000 a month, now they do 40,000, 45,000 a month. So it's like a 4x. No, math. 5 and 1 half x increase in revenue, which I think is kind of cool. OK, so here are the levers that you can control today. And the reason I think this is significant is that I can't remember who I said this to, but I said it on a daily call. And I was like, I'm going to write that down and say that again another day. So it sounds good. But the only thing that you need to do to change these things is a hard conversation. And you're going to have that hard conversation either way. It's either going to be with your customers or your spouse and your landlord about how you can't afford rent and you can't bring any bread home. Like, who do you want to have it with? I personally wouldn't want to have it with Layla. I'd be very happy to explain to Mrs. Johnson that because of the nature of the business, in order for me to give her exceptional service, I can no longer follow through with the original agreement that I had. And you have to accept responsibility. I made a mistake. I didn't understand the business the way I do now. In order for us to be profitable, you like this, Jim, right? Well, we have to make a profit in order to stay open. I always said that I talked to an accountant. Because no one thinks that accounts are trying to make money. So you're like, my accountant said that we don't have a profit margin. And they're like, oh, OK. Well, then that sounds serious because they talk to an accountant. So don't say you talk to your business coach. He's trying to make more money. So talk to your accountant. So the five things that you can control, your price, your billing cycle, the number of sessions, your capacity per session, and then your cost per session. So let's break each one down. So no, not all five are necessarily going to apply to you. So some of you guys might have two or three of these checked off the right way, which is great. But if you still have two that aren't, it means you have room to make your gym more profitable. If right now you see every all five of these check marks and all of them are unchecked, then you have a lot of work to do. And it means, good news, you have tons more profit you can make in your gym. So first thing, large group pricing. This kind of has two pieces to it. One is what I've seen across 1,100 whatever markets is at the price point, the sweet spot is about $150 to $200 a month. You can sometimes get away with like 219-ish. Depending on, Max Lone is the only person who can never listen to this because he's in the Bay Area, like downtown, and it's just like his rent's $15,000 a month. If your rent is $15,000 a month, you can tweak these numbers a little bit. But if it's not, then this is your number. Now that's for large group. Everyone has different definitions of what large group is. Large group should be, at least in my opinion, 16 people. Minimum. If you start getting into 12, you might as well just start moving other things around, which I'll get into. Because 12 people, it actually makes sense to have a price month that's like $300 a month. But anyone who's had a $300 a month price point understands that 80% of your customers are reaching for it, and 20% of your customers would just like more service. And so you get into this place that's like this no mans land in terms of pricing. So $150, $200 a month tends to be the sweet spot for this. If you are not there, raise your prices. That's it. Like it won't affect anything. It won't affect anything at all. You already know this because you signed up people on the back of the challenge, and they signed up fine at $200 a month. So the first step is to do that. The second step is to increase the price on the other people. Now I'm going to use Kirk as an example again. Is Kirk here still? Oh, he went on to interview. Classic. OK. So I'll just tell you what Kirk's numbers were. Is that Mrs. Kirk? It is Mrs. Kirk. Thank you. So the average EFT that Kirk had was $80 a month. Give or take? Yeah. About $80 a month at his gym. And by the end of the phone call, we went from $80 to $160. So we doubled it. And he lost three people? Yeah, not very many. He doubled his EFT. Now to make you feel a little bit better about it, one of my locations in Huntington Beach was having a certain, I had a stick on my ass. And so basically, I was just, I was fed up with group fitness and wanted to just make this only semi-private facility, whatever I was being an idiot. And so at the time, we had our large group at that location at $99 a month. It wasn't actually, it was probably $120 as the aggregate average, but it was in that neighborhood that I'm telling you to move out of. And so I just had a town hall meeting. And I said, hey guys, everything is $2.99 a month or more. And you get two free weeks on me to try it out. We're going to be doing semi-privates. You'll love it. And sorry. And I was even like, I still had multiple locations at this point. And so I wasn't in the gym. I didn't have the belly to belly. Even with that, tripling the price, I lost 30% of my members, but I tripled the price. That's why I don't like $2.99 as a price point. I've learned a lot of mistakes. But just as an example to you, if you're at $100, you can easily bump by $12 a week and say it as $12 a week. No one's going to shake whatever the saying is. No one's going to bat an eyelash at that. Because what we don't understand is that we're always in kind of a, especially you can be in a scarcity mindset, it's like when you're shopping for buildings, you're always like, man, the landlord has all the leverage. But when you're a landlord, I know because I have locations, you're like, shit, I really want this guy to rent. Because otherwise he's just sitting there. And so you're always thinking the other person has the upper hand, which is just interesting to me from a rental standpoint. But it works the same way here. Because the people who are at your gym, it's like, I don't want to find another gym. I like the people here. I like the trainers. It's convenient on my way to work. They've already made this ingrained pattern. For them to change it would be a huge cost. And so most people are willing to bear the burden if you have additional reasons for that. So I'll get into some of the reasons in a little bit. Fundamentally, just changing your price will just make you more money. And basically no one will leave. And so what the pricing model should look like is having two priced tiers. And for those of you who don't have high ticket yet, then it would just be one. And not do the classic, like, well, I've got six memberships, one's at 129, one's at 149. And I've got this other one that's super special at 169. I'm like, OK, cool. So why don't we just scrap all those, keep the 169, and then make your next level 500 to 700. That's the range for the second level. Because customers tend to be fractal. What I mean by that is 80, 20. So 80% of your customers are going to be the base. That's going to be your large group. But 20% of those people have, does everyone, right now, you can think of those customers that you have that you love, and their cards always go through. They always show up with a smile. They have the nice escalade. She always wears the new Lululemon. You know what I'm talking about, that person? Right. Great. She can afford 500 bucks a month. And she's happy to pay it. You just haven't told her about it yet. It's awesome. And what happens is if you have, let's say, 100 people at 150 just for math sake, and now you have 15,000 a month there, and you take 20 of them out and then get them to pay $600 a month, that's 12 grand a month from that 20%. It's cool, right? I think it's cool. But you can almost double the revenue of your facility by taking out that 20%, and that's why value ladders exist. Is that you want to give the opportunity to the person who wants to pay more money to give you more money and get better service. Because what happens is that really affluent customer, sometimes will just, I remember this. I had this girl who I loved. She was like, she always paid. She would come once or twice a week. And she was always chipper and just great. And then one day she was like, hey, I'm going to cancel. And I was like, why? Why are you leaving? You're the best. Why would you leave? She's like, oh, I'm going to go to a personal trainer. And I was like, how much is he? She was like, I think it's $1,200 a month. And she was like, I was like, you think it's $1,200 a month? I was like, shh. But I had already lost it. You know what I mean? And so that was a huge lesson to me. I was like, she literally wanted to pay me more. And I just didn't give her the opportunity. So don't make that mistake. OK. So here's the why. I think I covered most of this. 20 person. Oh, yeah. OK, so the example of what I'm trying to show here with the large group is that the issue that Josh ran into, that a lot of cross-stitch run-ins who is that if you're doing a lot of these levers kind of work together, but if you're doing unlimited memberships, and you have your EFT price at, let's say, 100, and you have 120 EFTs, and you have 20 people per session, which a lot of you guys, that's around where your max is. Not some of you, but a lot of you guys, that's where your max per session is. If you have 120 EFTs, and they're billing it $100 a month, and you can only take it on 20, and it's unlimited, your full capacity, you're doing $12,000 a month. You're negative. Like, it literally doesn't make sense if you just write it out what it's going to look like. Maybe you squeeze to 140 people. But the point is, even if you were to hypothetically fill your gym up, the people right now who live off challenge money is because you're selling each of those session spots for more money than your EFT is. That's why you have to keep doing it to make your gym work, is because your EFT is not high enough. And that should just be a big alarm for you of, well, then I need to raise my EFT. Because if it makes more sense for me to only service challengers, then that's a flawed model. Cool? OK. I think I'm making the point. Yeah, so you basically reach the cap before it's profitable. You can see the jump rope story for that. And then I cut for the pricing tiers. OK. That was number one. I know these are kind of nitty gritty, but I think everyone liked those. Is that valuable? OK. Second thing is billing cycle. You've heard me say this over and over again. I still don't think half of you have done this. Have you noticed that we're weekly billing? Why? Because we get more money. And it's easier. So I have this fundamental belief that if I were to charge someone $500 a month versus $119 a week, it sounds like so much less. And I can break that down into a lifestyle change so much more easily than $500 a month. Because they see $500 a month and they're thinking rent, car, gym. But if I'm like $119, I can break this down in terms of like, how much have you spent on clothes and not worn? You know what I mean? You can break that down really easily. And I don't know if you guys know this. But the average gym, this is just a report, net profit, it runs 8% to 12% margins per year. And probably a lot of you guys have seen, like you've looked at your tax report at some point, and we're like, we did $200 grand. I took home $25. Right? It sucks. But the point is, is that when you match your inflow to your outflow, this is something you can do tomorrow. You don't even need to change your prices. But I would encourage you to do both at the same time, because it just sounds totally different. Like if you're at $100 a month now, and I say, hey guys, we're changing on our service so that you get a better experience, because we want everyone to have a better experience. So now we're going to do semi-person, like you can call it semi-personal training, whatever. And so we're actually switching everyone to weekly. And so it's actually going to be $49 a week. It went from $100 to $49. And you're going to go from unlimited, which is not valuable, and I'll cover that in a second, to three sessions a week. It's like, oh, wow. That's cool. Now everybody has more attention. But what we just did is double the price. So the beauty of this is that, who here pays payroll every other week? Right. OK. But then you get, but you bill monthly. So if you bill monthly, you have 12 inflows. And if you have payroll every other week, you have 13 outflows. So one of those entire payrolls you eat. That's just like you taking your wallet out and giving it for nothing. And so I feel like this doesn't even make gyms more money. I feel like it just gets them back to where they should have been to begin with, to be honest. But if you go from monthly to weekly, bi-weekly, or in a 28-day cycle, basically going off of weeks instead of months, you get that 0.3 back. So there's 4.3 weeks per month. And that's because four months of the year, we get five Mondays, or five Tuesdays, or whatever it is, whatever day you're billing. And so during four months of the year, if you do weekly, you get five billings instead of four. And so the beauty of that is that, one, it matches the inflows. But for a gym that changes nothing, you increase your gross revenue by 8% with no extra work. Why do I think that's cool is that if the average gym is taking home 10% margins, we just doubled your take home. Like, one change. Doubled your take home didn't change anything else. And for me, I have this belief that it's easier to shear sheep than skin it. I haven't heard that saying, but I heard it and I like it. But it works well to switch to this when you're also making that change. I'm just repeating this again. It's just that if you are going to make this change in your own pricing, it works well to do them both in tandem. Because it seems it comes off as though you're really thinking about it. Like, we've really thought this through. OK, did everyone get that one? It's a simple thing to do, but most people don't do it. I'll say one more thing on this. So I said there's three different billing cycles that you can follow. You can do 28-day. You can do bi-weekly, or you can do weekly. The advantages of I would probably go either 28-day cycles, because that's from an administrative standpoint. It's a lot easier. You only have one billing cycle to look after. And you don't have people saying, hey, I missed this week. Can you credit that week forward or whatever, which is kind of a pain. And you have to, for us, just have them initial that unless someone's leaving for a vacation, missing of anything is ever accounted for. So that just has to be a clear expectation that said if you do do weekly, do do. But 28-day cycles is the easiest thing to switch to. But I like weekly from a sales standpoint, because it just sounds so cheap. It's like, eh, it's $49 a week. What are you going to do? Plan to finish it out of here. That's for Gil. OK. So sessions daily. So this is the one that we crushed with Kirk. So Kirk had eight sessions or nine a day when he and I were talking. Eight or nine a day. And it doesn't even sound like much. But if you have four in the morning and four in the afternoon, you've got eight sessions. And so the easiest way to do this math, I don't want you to get like two. This is the mathiest one of all of them, is I like to run 80% margins minimum on any service that we sell. Because that's your gross margin off of what you're selling. But then you still have all the costs of doing business. You still have rent. You still have your administrative costs. You still have marketing, other stuff. But the thing that you're actually selling, you should be making minimum 80% profit on. Because the mistake that a lot of people will make is like, well, if I open up the session and I get one more person, I'm making an extra $100 a month. It doesn't work that way. Because you have all the other costs associated with that. And you already know that it doesn't work because you're not making money. So just believe me on this. OK. So 80% growth profit off of service. So we had this example. Now, 160 classes, this is per month. So this is assuming the old model, not if you'd switch to weekly, but just so we can keep parallel. If you had 160 sessions, that would be 40 sessions a week. Which is really like, if you're open Monday through Friday and you have seven a day, that's 35. And then let's say you have two on Saturday and two on Sunday. Boom. It's not hard to get there. We took Kirk from 160 classes to 100 or 110. But I thought we took you down to five. Changed to five. OK. Cool. So we went from eight down to five for Kirk. So we knocked out three a day. And all you have to do to see the math is, if you have a $12,000 month EFT, 160 classes you're making, 75 bucks a class. Just simple math. You're just like, how much you gross? How many classes? That's how many you make per class. And I got pay somebody. Now, I include payroll taxes in this. So if you don't include payroll taxes, you should, because that's included. But in California, it was 1.22. That's what I always knew. It was 1.22 times whatever their hourly pay was what I was actually paying. And so if you're paying 20, it's 25. And Kirk had some people who was paying 30, and some people who was paying 20. And so it added up. But with this, he was making $50, because we took this out. This is equals 50, right? 70 minus 25 over 75, which means a 66% margin. And you might see 66% and be like, oh, that's really close. Every percentage point that you gain on that is enormous. It's huge. In terms of how much you actually need to change the, if you actually try and play with these numbers to get to 80, this number needs to double. It's huge. So for me, and so you can run this through as your litmus test of, do I have too many classes, or is my price too low for what I have? And we have this great Excel sheet that does all this for you. But I wanted to give this as a simple, hopefully, example. The easiest thing to do, the number one thing to increase profitability, is that if you have 160 classes, and we drop it to just, let's say, 2 in the morning, 3 in the afternoon, and we have 5 per day now, then that five classes a day gets us, and then I usually would maybe do one on Friday nights, or take Friday nights off, because your trainers don't want to work on Friday night either. And so that way you have four times five, you got 20, you got four, or two in the morning, or three in the morning, depending on how you want to do it. So you got 23, and then maybe two on the weekend, you got 25, times four you get 100. So if you save 60 sessions, and your cost per class is 25 bucks, that's real money. And if you normally take home $2,000 a month, if you take home $3,200 just from that one change, we just increase your take home by 50%. It's kind of big, right? And then when you combine that with increasing how much you're charging, then it gets really, really juicy, and then you start making like six or eight grand a month. It's pretty neat. So here's the example. So I'll give you an example. If your old price is $12,000 a month, and then you have 110 classes, and we drop it from $1.6 to $1.10. So that's a pretty easy change. I gave us 10 more classes, assuming you're going to get that 0.3. So if we did 25 a week, like that last three days, we get us 10. Then we make $109 per class, just by making that one switch. Nothing else. Nothing else changed. We didn't change the prices. All we did is we dropped from 160 classes to 110. And now we're making $109 per session, instead of $75 per session. And we do the math out, where it's 77% margins. But you see, we dropped the classes by a third. And we still didn't hit 80%. So when you have that goal of 80%, it should be your gold standard that you want to try and get over. And then when I would see that, I wouldn't think, hmm, I need to come more classes. I would probably think, OK, I probably need to increase my price a little bit, which is obviously the situation here. And then, oh, look, I thought ahead. And if you had a new price, and I'm assuming you lost 10 EFTs, 10%, which is bigger than anyone else who's made an increase has lost, and you did it with $167. Now you've got an 80% margin. You're making $136 a class. Does that make sense? Cool, right? OK. Does everyone get this? This is probably the biggest thing that I see, especially when someone's starting out. Like 90%, even people who are just opening their gym, they're like, I'm opening up with six sessions today. I'm like, do you don't have customers? Oops. One of my screens turned off. OK. You don't even have customers. So even if you had, like, people were like, I have 50 EFTs. I'm like, why do you have 160 sessions a month? You have 50 EFTs. Like, for you to serve as 50 EFTs, A, it should be three days a week. So now you only have to do 50 every other day. And so I'd recommend just starting with Monday, Wednesday, Friday. And just tell everyone to go Monday, Wednesday, Friday. And now you don't have Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday, payroll you have to worry about. And then if you're doing three days a week with that, and you have 50 people, and you have a 20-person capacity, you only need, like, three sessions. So now we have, like, 12 sessions a week. Now you have the profitable business. And then as you scale up and get more customers, then you add sessions. But you scale as you stay profitable. Does that make sense? Because what we used to do, I did this. I'm saying, like, I did not do this. Like, don't make my mistake. I started with seven sessions. And I did the same thing. I was like, somebody was like, well, if you had a session at 7 o'clock at night, or 7 30 at night, I could make it. And I was like, well, we do have a 7 30 class. And then Alex came and did personal training from 7 30 to 8 o'clock every night. You know what I mean? And her and her sister came. And I did that for months. And I was like, this is stupid. Alex was stupid. Bad decision. I realized at one point, I was like, I'm getting $5 for every hour. I was like, I would pay so much to have this hour back and just not be here right now. You just got to learn. So those are the first three. Number four is the cap of the sessions. So this is how you're actually designing your fulfillment. Now, I did this in order of ease of change. Easiest thing to change is your price. Second easiest is your billing cycle. Third easiest is how many sessions are scheduled change. Easiest thing to do with the schedule change when you do need to change it is just say it's because of the season. So it's like, hey, what are we in now? June? Hey, summer hours. And then if it's August, you're like back to school hours. And then if it's November, you're like New Year hours. We've got spring hours. It really doesn't matter. You just change the hours. And the thing is, I've never had anyone ask for when they come back, ever. No one ever asked. They're like, oh, spring hours. But six months later, they're like, we didn't switch back from spring. He didn't switch back. That dog. No one ever asked. And so this is probably the fourth one. It's probably the fourth hardest to do. So if you only did three, do the first three. Everyone should do the first three immediately. Number four is, and this is really a just and less situation. So if you're really running into capacity issues, you're like, dude, we're selling on the streets. That's what they're doing. They're selling outside of their gym because they want to sell their gym. And they can only take 16 people per session. So what you do here is, if you have 60 minute sessions, and a lot of people put 15 minutes in between, most of us have gotten rid of the 15 minutes because you realize, huh, I have to stay way longer. That's silly. But a lot of people are still doing 60 minute full sessions. I almost always did 45 minutes. And if you think about how you're selling something, if a dentist said, hey, I can remove your tooth, and it's going to take me five minutes, and it's too grand, you're like, what? Too grand for five minutes? And then it's a famous joke, but it's like, would you prefer? It takes an hour? And you're like, oh. Because it's silly because you're selling a result. And so if someone's like, they're only 30 minutes, it's like, do you want to work out for three hours? And then they're like, oh, no. It's like, well, if I can get you to where you're trying to go as a general population person trying to lose weight by moving your body and lifting weights 30 minutes a day, do you think I can help you? Yeah, absolutely. So it depends on how extreme your situation is with area. I know what the cocoa fits. So we have Tim Khalees here with cocoa fits. They've got 60 locations. And for them, they have like 500 square feet of usable space because they've got all these machines. And so what we did with them was we just did 30 minute blocks. And so with 30 minute blocks, and we had people on the treadmills turned off doing stuff in groups, we were able to do 12 people every 30 minutes, which gives us 24 people per hour, which increased the capacity of their facilities. Does that make sense? So even if you went from 60 to 45, it's going to decrease your payroll because of the actual hours, true hours, that someone's working. One. Two, if you're doing 45, you can usually pop another class in. And remember, this is the opposite problem. This is if we have somebody who's at full capacity and just needs to make more money, then we need to increase capacity. And so obviously the last one is the 30 minute thing. But once you go to 30 minute classes, and you're doing three sessions a week, you can do a lot out of a facility if you're doing 30 minute classes. If you had four hours, that's eight sessions that you can potentially work in. And even if you have a small facility with 16 people max, you can do 32 people an hour times eight. It's 240 people serviced in the afternoon. That's why I've always been a big proponent of, you can make as much money as you want from one facility if you're willing to just have a couple hard conversations. OK. Yes, and this is the last piece, which hopefully I've already harped home a million times. But if you're selling limited, the only way that I've seen that I was OK with selling unlimited is if someone does a year paid in full upfront. And I kind of did it as an exclusive thing. And sometimes you want cash flow for whatever reason. Or you want to do a big campaign, you don't feel like taking out of your account. I get it. That's the only time I give someone a limited. And then it becomes this kind of exclusive thing. You give them a little wristband, put them on the wall as like a VIP. And it's a paid in full with no discount. You just paid for the year. And the reason that paid in full has got a bad name in the fitness industry is because there are a lot of people who would say, buy six months, get six months free, or buy a year, get a year free, or crazy stuff like that, and then do it at $100 a month. I mean, sell $1,000 for two years. It's like, well, yeah, you're not going to make any money that way. But I'm cool with paid in full. If someone wants to pay in full for legacy tomorrow, y'all can all pay in full tomorrow. By all means, we're looking at lifetime value. And the thing is that at the end of that contract, they're still in continuity. So it's like, you're paying full for a year, you're still going to start billing again 12 months later. So for me, if I decrease the risk of them leaving, it's guaranteed that I now have the next 12 months. And it's cash in my pocket today, which I can make more money off of than cash in my pocket 12 months from now. So I do take a little bit of a stance of like, I'm not against paid in fulls. I'm against paid in fulls at a discount. Does that make sense? So if you want to do it, enhance the value for that and make it like an exclusive group, but limit it. You know what I mean? Just for when you, if you want an extra cash pop or something like that. But for the most part, 95% of the people who are at your facility should be at three days a week. Because that three days a week, three days a week, Monday, Wednesday, Friday, three days a week, Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday, right? We just doubled the facility. Now, if you're doing that and you're going to switch people over when you have the conversation and when you're getting challengers in the door, always start with, so Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday work? Start there. Because if you say does, like, everyone wants to do Monday, Wednesday, Friday, like if you start with Monday, Wednesday, Friday, like, do you want Monday, Wednesday, Friday, Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday? They're like, I'll take Monday, Wednesday, Friday. So start with Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday, and only the people who can't make it give those people Monday, Wednesday, Friday. Does that make sense? I know because I did this and I did the wrong way the first time. So don't do that. Does that make sense for number four? OK. Number five, and this is the last one that, like, it's, it's the last one. I don't, I don't like changing this one. It's just like, if I'm in an extreme, totally dire circumstance, at the end of the day, like, if the business is not profitable, there will be no business, and then no one gets paid, right? And so if you, like, in Kirk's situation, he had, I think, he had two trainers at 20 an hour, which, with taxes, he was paying 25. He had one trainer at $30 an hour with, with taxes, he was paying 35 per hour. And then I think he had one intern at, like, 15. And lo and behold, half of his class was being taught by the $30 an hour trainer, right? And so when we just said, hey, these are the rules. This is how we have to do it. I mean, it sucks. Like, I'm not like, you know what I mean? Like, you just have to have, like, hard conversations. That's why I say, like, you're either going to have a hard conversation with your landlord and your wife, or you're going to have with a trainer, right? But if, if you, like, the only way to navigate this conversation, just in my experience, is just being honest. It's just saying, like, hey, they're like, where, this is not like, like, yogurt store owners who own, like, frozen yogurt. Not all, like, they're not offering the person behind the yogurt counter 60 grand a year with benefits. You know what I mean? I'm not saying, I'm not equating a trainer to a person behind the yogurt counter. But to some extent, there are some limitations within the business model. And this is something that I'm actively working on, to try and figure out how I can get everyone's salary to, like, hire. It's something that, like, I really care about. Because we're prolific enough at gym once that we pay above industry average, and I really like that. But I'm giving you my experience from this. And this is why I'm saying this is the last change. Most times, if you do the first four, you never even need to get to this. But when we were in, you know, Dire Straight with Kirk, it was like, you just have to have the hard conversations. Like, either we close this gym, or we have to change payroll, we have to decrease classes. And, like, we got to get this thing back under control. So we have a profit we can build from there. That makes sense. It's not a fun conversation. But the baseline, mind you, I'm only, I'm going to pull out Dustin and Emily, Nate Pirch on my seven-figure call with them. They're like, we found out if we pay people more, they were happier. And I was like, that's cool. So what did you guys go from? They're paying their trainers $10 an hour. And so, and they're a seven-figure gym. That's like the allergy for them than I am. But for me, I think you start people at 15 virtually every market. Some people are like, why am I in Southern California? All my gyms are in Southern California. Like, there's tons of people who are willing to work for that. $15 an hour, especially like, if you give them the opportunity to do other things like the internal plays, the whole point is to incentivize them to do other things. Right? But does that make sense? Okay, it's not my favorite one, but I'd just like to cover it because it is an important point because if we can decrease, if you're in a position right now where you're at 25 an hour, you have 160 sessions, going from 25 to 15 and then dropping from 160 to 100, literally will drop your payroll like we did with Kirk by like seven grand a month. And that was it. We didn't change anything else. And then we increased the prices double. And that's how we made $8,500 a month appear magically. Right? And then all of a sudden, the gym's more fun, right? Because you make a profit. End result. So when you put all these five levers together, how am I doing on time? I'm doing good. Okay, so when you put all these levers together, what are happening is that we can two to four X capacity depending on what you're decreasing in terms of how many sessions you're doing per day and then the duration of each of those sessions. The next thing is that if you increase the prices and you decrease the capacity, increases the revenue per session, right? Because even before when I showed you the class example, this one, we didn't change. Did I change? Yeah, even with this, when I didn't change the price, we still increased the revenue per session by 40 bucks per session without changing the price. But when you change the price and decrease the sessions, then you can double the revenue or more per session, all right? And then now on top of that, so besides increasing your capacity, increasing the amount of revenue per session total revenue overall, you're also decreasing your overall cost and sometimes by a lot most times. Does that make sense? Okay, so let me see if I have a good point on this. Okay, there we go. Most people in here will not do what I just said. They will think, I should do that. Or I can't do that because I'm a special snowflake and my market is different and my people are different and you just don't understand how it's like in blah, blah, blah, Texas and we're in a really hard area. My player, yeah, I hit hard by the economy, but wow, whatever. All right, kind of like what Myron said before, it's like I choose to make my own economy. Doesn't matter what president's here, rich stay rich, poor stay poor, because what's here, right? So you can choose to do that, you can choose not to. But if you have the opportunity right now, if you were to change these things and double your take home or triple your take home, would you do it? What's the risk? The fear is that everyone's gonna leave, right? That's the irrational fear. Is they're like, I'm gonna tell my clients I'm gonna switch from $100 a month to $49 a week and they're all gonna leave. I tripled my price, was not there, most of them didn't like me at the time. I didn't really understand how management worked. I was like, whatever, deal with the trainers. People used to run through the lobby when I was in it because they didn't wanna talk to me. I had the reverse situation where everyone loved my trainers and didn't wanna talk to me. Because they knew that if they sat down with me, I was gonna get money out of them. That was just what it was. I've changed a lot since then. But they're like, Alex, I have a question. I'm like, sit down, Dorothy. How are your goals going? And then all of a sudden, you just gave me $1,000. I'm like, whatever. So, but my point is, if you had the opportunity to do it, the only thing that's holding you back is really just fear. And me doing that in that horrible situation where I had $99 a month people switching them to $299 a month with all these facts that they didn't really even like me, they even want me to make money. You know what I mean? We still lost 30%. That's the real number. We still made more money. And we had less people. And the people who left, guess who left? People sucked. You know what I mean? Sucked the life out of everyone. My trainers were really nervous about it. They were like, dude, they called it the great purge. They were like, dude, it was the best. They're like, oh, Maria left. Maria was always texting me pictures of her poop and telling me that she was in digestion and like asking me to fix her meal plan. She never even, and every weekend I see her at Cheesecake Factory. I'm like, girl, like, come on. Like I made your meal plan 19 times, right? Because you didn't care. It was $9 a month and it was wherever, right? But if you decide to take these steps, like at least take two or three, like the easiest ones to do, raising prices, changing billing cycles, super easy, cutting classes a little bit more difficult, but still do with the change in season, never an issue. They do it at ice cream stores. It's not a big deal, all right? But my recommendation is the final point is that if you make all the changes, do them all at once and say it because you hired an accountant and they said that you needed to correct course immediately or you wouldn't have survived another year. It's just, it's just like, hey, this, and the biggest thing to do here is that you have to accept full responsibility because it is yours. It's no one else's. You have to be like, Dorothy, I messed up. I can no longer withhold my end of the agreement. We made an agreement that you would pay $99 a month and I would give you as many sessions as humanly possible, right? And for some reason that didn't work out financially for me. And so I'm really sorry. And hopefully you guys have enough flow that's coming in from the stuff that we're doing with us that you're like, listen, the gym's picked up. I can no longer hold up my end of this. I desperately want you to stay, but I'm not going to try and force you to or increase your price without your permission. And so I just wanted to have this conversation with you to see where you're at, where your goals, and then to see if adding extra $12 a week is something that we can do. And still keep you as a part of the family. That make sense? Most times Dorothy's like, well, I'm really glad you approached me like this. And it's a little out of the blue, but okay, I'll see if I can swing it. You're like, Dorothy, let's just do this right now. Actually, you know, I'm in the sales mode. You know what I mean? But just confront it and then sign it up. And then in future contracts, it's nice to have just a line that says, you can increase prices. I never would increase prices on someone without their permission, but it's nice to just not have to resign contracts and stuff. This is a tip. But yeah, if you're going to do this, go all in with it. Oh, cool, I had more slides. Okay, yeah, so this is where I give you conviction. Okay, great. So how do you actually do this? Here are the steps. Man, I'm great. Okay, so depending on the size of your gym and how many people you have, push everything to a town hall meeting in person for Q&A about the changes or make a video to your Facebook group and push it as a text to say, hey, you should actually all watch this video. So I made this really emotional video of me sitting on one of our tires at the gym just having this heart to heart with my Facebook group. And I just texted everyone to say, watch this video. That's how I tripled prices. So you could probably do it more tactfully than that, but I did it like that and it was okay. So you could probably do it better than I did. But admit fault and be absolutely genuine and be like, hey, I want to over-deliver service and we're seeing CrossFit go out of business left and right. And the reason for that is because the model that I was originally told would work is actually broken. And I found out this from an accountant and so we're changing course and I accept full responsibility. It's on me. Explain how you don't want anyone to leave and if anyone has questions, you're happy to meet with them individually. And at that point, if someone does, and if someone's like, I have questions, a lot of times they have questions and they just want you to be like, it's going to be okay. And they're like, okay. That's all, like it's literally it. Like you're just like, it's going to be all right. They're like, all right. You're just going to pay me 50 bucks a month more and you're going to enjoy it $50 a month more. Right? Have you ever had two wines, $1, $13, $130, same wine? They like this one more? Same thing at this place. And the fourth thing is a company with a more personalized approach for training three days a week and painting walls in uniforms. Those are kind of a little more tactical stuff. If you get all your trainers a polo or something has a collar on it and you get those swishy pants and you paint the walls that have all the scuff marks on it and the bathroom, always clean the bathroom and say that you're transitioning to a more personalized approach so that you can serve people at a higher level and get really emotional. I actually cried on my video. It was dope. It was staged. I knew I was going to cry. I thought about my mom and I was like, oh. But I got into that because I was like, I just want to get you guys results. But you have to face the reality that this isn't going to work. And so if you approach it from that angle and you really do genuinely care, you want them to get like, you should wait. I think I have a slide for this. Right. I already told you all this. And yeah, just so you know, with every other gym that we've done this with, I get to see anyone lose more than 5% because no one's doing a tripling of their EFT like I did. So like if you go from like $110 a month to $167, nothing's going to happen. You'll be fine. But like I said, with this data, you can make your own decision. But the sooner you fix your model, the sooner you make more money. That makes sense? Cool. Does everyone who doesn't have profit owners believe that they can do this? It seems attainable. Cool. Thank you. Appreciate that. Does everyone feel like they understand what they know and how to do it? Last note. I used to say that if you're not at full capacity, you don't need to worry about this until you are. I'm not saying like fully against that stance. It's just that there is like, that used to be my belief, but just seeing enough people like be there, be there, be there and then not be profitable and then have to do this and then be profitable. I'm like, I don't know why we need to wait for that. So I think I've transitioned my own conviction in terms of like, this will make your gym more profitable. You should do this.
